payload.summary: Reviewed all 7 amendments against the actual code. Each amendment identifies a genuine issue — ranging from undocumented spec deviations, missing test coverage, timestamp correctness bugs, unnecessary double-writes, implicit invariants, atomicity violations, and incomplete migration of timestamp-based identity heuristics. The most critical are the atomicity issue (fr-21-97db243d) where legacy repair writes can leave artifacts in an inconsistent state, and the incomplete read-path migration (fr-21-fc1d9825). All amendments are accepted.

votes.rationale: Verified in code: unique_terminal_replay_match (line ~2748), is_legacy_start_for (line ~2606), and is_superseded_legacy_start (line ~2596) are all present and actively used in update_task_run's fallback path at lines ~2989-3015. The fallback filters by started_at and uses unique_terminal_replay_match to resolve legacy completions. fail_legacy_runless_entries only marks runless Running entries as Failed — it does not eliminate these code paths for entries that already have terminal outcomes from the legacy era. The spec deviation is real and undocumented. Either complete the removal or formally document the retention with rationale.

fail_legacy_runless_entries mutates on-disk state on every record_task_run_start and update_task_run call. I confirmed the function modifies entries in-place (setting outcome to Failed, adding outcome_detail, setting finished_at) and triggers an immediate write_task_runs. The three proposed test scenarios are well-targeted and necessary: (1) verify legacy runless Running entries are failed with correct detail text 'superseded: run_id is now required (upgrade repair)', (2) verify terminal entries are untouched, (3) verify the new run proceeds normally after repair. A migration function touching persistent state without tests is a clear gap.

Confirmed in code: record_task_run_start passes started_at to fail_legacy_runless_entries, and update_task_run passes finished_at. The function sets entry.finished_at = Some(now) where 'now' is the caller's timestamp, not the current clock time or a per-entry safe value. If a legacy entry's started_at is after the caller's timestamp (clock skew, out-of-order writes), this creates finished_at < started_at — an impossible timeline. The fix of using max(legacy_entry.started_at, now) is straightforward and defensively correct. No downside.

Verified the double-write pattern: at lines ~2820-2822 and ~2945-2947, if legacy_repaired is true, entries are immediately written via write_task_runs. Then record_task_run_start writes again at line ~2924, and update_task_run writes again at line ~3149. This is both wasteful I/O and creates a transient inconsistent on-disk state (legacy repairs present, but actual operation not yet recorded). Since this is directly coupled with the atomicity issue in fr-21-97db243d, deferring the legacy repair to the final write would resolve both issues simultaneously.

Confirmed same_attempt at model.rs:~298-311 falls back to started_at matching when either run_id is None (the wildcard arm). After fail_legacy_runless_entries marks all runless Running entries as terminal, live non-terminal entries should always have run_id. But this invariant is implicit — nothing enforces it. Adding a debug_assert or explanatory comment is low-cost and high-value: it catches invariant violations early in development and documents the expected state for future maintainers. The fallback remains necessary for the read-path collapse of already-terminal legacy entries, so removal isn't appropriate — but documenting when it applies is.

This is the most serious issue. Confirmed: fail_legacy_runless_entries and its immediate write happen at lines ~2818-2822 (record_task_run_start) and ~2943-2947 (update_task_run), BEFORE validation checks. In record_task_run_start, the duplicate-finalized checks at lines ~2840-2865 can return Err after the legacy write. In update_task_run, the missing/ambiguous checks at lines ~2948-3038 can similarly fail. The service layer reconciliation at service.rs:~439-452 and ~568-584 only runs after the adapter call succeeds. This means task-runs.ndjson gets mutated while status.json and journal.ndjson retain their pre-repair state — a cross-artifact inconsistency. The repair must be deferred until success is confirmed, or coordinated through the service layer.

Confirmed: the read path in model.rs:~497-582 retains legacy_group_accepts_completion and unique_open_legacy_group_index, which match run_id=None rows to terminal rows by started_at. This collapse pipeline feeds snapshot reconciliation (service.rs:~164-170 via collapse_task_run_attempts) and bead audit queries (fs.rs:~3161 via collapse_task_run_attempts). The code even has a comment documenting this as a 'read-only backward-compat path'. While the comment explains the intent, the amendment's concern is valid: a legacy/corrupt runless row sharing a started_at with a different named run can be folded into the wrong group. fail_legacy_runless_entries only mitigates write-path issues; existing terminal runless entries (legitimately completed legacy entries) still flow through timestamp-based grouping. The remove-runless-matching change is genuinely incomplete on the read side.
